citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: A B S T R A C T For nearly 30 years, UV-induced free radical polymerization has been used to modify the surface chemistry of polyethersulfone (PES) membranes, films, and coatings. The initial mechanism for the grafting process was introduced in the 1990s and supported without direct evidence of covalent bond formation. Since then, claims of ‘grafting from’ membranes in the literature rely on similar evidence using a combination of gravimetry, atten­uated total reflectance infrared spectroscopy, contact angle goniometry, water sorption, and/or water per­meance. While these techniques provide evidence of the presence of a polymer coating, they do not provide direct evidence of covalent bond formation between the grafted polymer and the PES membrane or support. Thus, it is possible to mischaracterize a physisorbed film as a chemically bonded grafted polymer. We use experimental data from a model system to examine two key areas: 1) the impact of experimental parameters (UV intensity, UV wavelength, grafting time, and monomer concentration) on the degree of grafting and 2) the limitations of the current literature-accepted techniques used to characterize surfaces functionalized by UVgrafting. NMR is introduced as a technique to quantitatively analyze grafting from a PES membrane and is compared directly to the literature-accepted techniques to expose ‘false positives’ that may lead to incorrect conclusions about ‘grafting from’.

Finally, we use 2D NMR techniques to present direct evidence of covalent bond formation from the UV-initiated reaction between a model PES monomer and a model methacrylate monomer in solution.

citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: 1. Introduction Surface modification of polymer membranes is a common approach to achieve desired surface properties while maintaining the bulk me­chanical properties of the membrane [1]. Two common approaches include (1) coating a thin layer that physically adheres to the surface [2–4] and (2) grafting a polymer layer to or from the base membrane that is covalently attached [5]. Adsorptive membranes grafted with polymeric ligands have been used in pharmaceutical purification [6], heavy metal remediation [7–11], and radioanalytical chemistry [12,13]. Other applications of polymer grafted membranes include mitigating membrane fouling during water treatment [14–17], enhancing blood compatibility [18,19], and improving the performance of membranes in energy devices [20,21]. These functional polymers can be synthesized and then attached to the membrane surface (‘grafting to’) [22–24], or grown from the membrane surface (‘grafting from’) [25]. Both classes of grafting are characterized by the formation of a covalent bond between the functional polymer and the membrane substrate. Of these two techniques, ‘grating from’ lends itself to a high grafting density, whereas ‘grafting to’ generally results in a lower grafting density due to diffusion limitations and steric hindrance during the grafting reaction [25–27].

content: as ‘grafting to’ generally results in a lower grafting density due to diffusion limitations and steric hindrance during the grafting reaction [25–27]. Common ‘grafting from’ methods include activating the membrane surface using ultraviolet (UV) light [28–32], plasma [33–36], or gamma rays [37–42] to create free radicals on the membrane surface which initiate a polymerization reaction, thereby ‘grafting from’ the mem­brane. Alternatively, alkyl halide solid-phase initiators can be installed directly on the membrane surface for subsequent controlled polymeri­zation reactions like surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymeri­zation (SI-ATRP) [43–47] or activators regenerated by electron transfer, a type of atom transfer radical polymerization (ARGET ATRP) [48,49].

citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: A popular and seemingly simple method to graft functional polymers from polyethersulfone (PES) and polysulfone membranes is UV-initiated free radical polymerization. In this method, membranes are either immersed or dipped in a monomer solution before UV irradiation. In the dip technique, the membrane is soaked in a monomer solution, removed, and then irradiated under UV light. In the immersion technique, the membrane is submerged in the monomer solution during irradiation [30,32]. The proposed mechanism for this grafting technique relies on the intrinsic reactivity of PES with UV light such that, when irradiated, free radicals are generated by cleavage near the sulfonyl group (-SO2) [29,30,50,51]. Presumably, the generated free radicals serve as solid-phase initiators to anchor the first monomer to the membrane surface which is followed by chain propagation. To increase the degree of grafting (i.e., the total mass of polymer attached to the membrane), a polymerization initiator like azoisobutylonitrile (AIBN) [52–55] or benzophenone is often employed [5,56,57]. After UV irradiation, the membranes are washed in a good solvent for the monomer (oftentimes DI water or ethanol) to remove unreacted monomer and physisorbed polymer. The rigor of the membrane washing procedures may include rinsing [30], mixing on a shaker table in a solvent for a few hours [58,59] or sequential washing in organic solvents [57,58]. To determine a sufficient washing time, Pieracci et al.

citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: table in a solvent for a few hours [58,59] or sequential washing in organic solvents [57,58]. To determine a sufficient washing time, Pieracci et al. [59] used attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) to track changes in the characteristic carbonyl peaks asso­ciated with the grafted poly(vinyl pyrrolidone). The carbonyl absor­bance remained unchanged beyond 2 h of soaking the membranes in DI water; therefore, it was chosen as an appropriate washing time [59]. After washing, membranes are characterized by several methods to understand changes in the membrane surface. Gravimetric analysis is used to measure the mass of polymer deposited on a surface [32,36,42,60]. Changes in the surface chemistry are analyzed using ATR-FTIR, where the appearance of characteristic peaks from functional groups on the polymer coating is used to support surface functionalization [28,30,39,41,42,56,60]. Similarly, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been used to quantify the surface concentration of elements before and after functionalization [18,37,61,62]. Contact angle goniometry is used to observe changes in the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface [32,39,41,56,60]. In some cases, changes in membrane morphology after functionalization are observed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) [37,39,41,60,63,64] or atomic force microscopy (AFM) [25,28,63].

citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: rphology after functionalization are observed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) [37,39,41,60,63,64] or atomic force microscopy (AFM) [25,28,63]. Finally, changes in the membrane performance such as water per­meance [28,32,37,39,60], selectivity [7,8], or flux decline [39,63] may be used as evidence to support functionalization. These techniques are widely used in the membrane literature and are reviewed elsewhere [25,65]. Importantly, these techniques only provide evidence of changes in surface chemistry or morphology which can be achieved by both phys­isorption and covalent tethering of a functional polymer to the mem­brane surface. These techniques do not provide direct evidence of covalent bond formation between the polymer coating and the mem­brane and, therefore, cannot be used to claim that the functional poly­mer was grafted to or from the membrane. Consider ATR-FTIR, a surface-sensitive measurement used to probe vibrational frequencies of chemical bonds at depths of 0.5–5 μm within the membrane depending on the ATR crystal [65,66]. Often, the appearance of characteristic peaks from the “grafted” polymer is used as an indicator of successful grafting. For example, when functionalizing PES membranes with methacrylate-based monomers, a strong carbonyl stretching (C––O) peak appears near a wavenumber of 1730 cm-1 [66,67].

citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: nctionalizing PES membranes with methacrylate-based monomers, a strong carbonyl stretching (C––O) peak appears near a wavenumber of 1730 cm-1 [66,67]. Commonly, researchers will use this peak alone to claim ‘grafting from’ the membrane, however methacrylate monomers are susceptible to thermal and UV-initiated homopolymerization; therefore, the pres­ence of C––O cannot distinguish between a covalently bound or phys­isorbed methacrylate polymer [25]. To directly confirm covalent attachment using ATR-FTIR, characteristic absorbances for the forma­tion of a new C–C bond must be observed after functionalization; how­ever, these C–C bonds are challenging to isolate as they appear in the fingerprint region of the PES polymer and overlap with the C–C peaks of the methacrylate homopolymer [68]. Similarly, the formation of a new SO2–C bond may be indistinguishable between reactants and products. The pristine PES membrane contains an SO2-aromatic bond, and the grafted PES would contain a new SO2-alkane bond. Therefore, it is challenging to directly observe the covalent attachment of the methac­rylate monomers to PES membranes using ATR-FTIR. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectroscopy (NMR) is an analytical technique that can be used to provide direct evidence of the covalent attachment of the polymer to the surface of membranes. NMR detects nuclei of non-zero angular momentum, typically 1H and 13C nuclei.

citation.title: Functionalizing polyethersulfone membranes: using NMR to avoid pitfalls when using UV-induced polymerization to 'graft from' surfaces

content: e of the covalent attachment of the polymer to the surface of membranes. NMR detects nuclei of non-zero angular momentum, typically 1H and 13C nuclei. To make an NMR measurement, the sample is placed in an applied magnetic field, which aligns the bulk magnetization vector of the sample (the equilibrium sum of the individual magnetic moments of each nucleus) parallel to the applied field. Then, a pulsed radiofrequency (RF) is applied, causing the magnetization vector of the sample to tilt at an angle relative to the applied magnetic field. As the magnetization vector relaxes to realign with the applied magnetic field, the precession of the vector is detected as current-induced upon a coil of wire in the NMR. Each chemically unique nucleus is associated with its own magnetiza­tion vector, and the detected free induction decay (FID) signal, after Fourier transform, gives rise to a spectrum with characteristic peaks for each chemically unique nucleus [69]. A monomer covalently coupled to the PES unit will exert a different chemical, and therefore magnetic, environment upon the 1H and 13C nuclei in PES. In the context of membrane characterization, this means that NMR can differentiate a PES unit covalently bonded to a monomer from an unmodified PES unit. Two-dimensional NMR techniques are useful tools to identify the attachment of 1H to 13C nuclei that are connected by one, two, or three bonds via the transfer of magnetization from the excited nucleus to the attached nucleus.

content: of 1H to 13C nuclei that are connected by one, two, or three bonds via the transfer of magnetization from the excited nucleus to the attached nucleus. Peaks obtained in Heteronuclear Single-Quantum Correlation (HSQC) or Heteronuclear Multiple-Quantum Correlation (HMQC) experiments indicate a one bond connection between the cor­responding 1H and 13C nuclei. The peaks obtained in the Heteronuclear Multiple-Bond Correlation (HMBC) experiment indicate a two or three bond connection [69]. In the membrane literature, NMR has been used to confirm the chemical modification of PES in solution prior to casting a membrane [70,71]. However, there are limited examples of using NMR to directly characterize PES or other polymer membranes after grafting a functional polymer from a solid porous membrane [72]. 1H NMR can be particularly advantageous for this application because it generates distinct peaks for 1H nuclei in the single bond versus double bond en­vironments, which will aid in distinguishing monomers from polymer chains [73]. A combination of 1H NMR and HMBC can identify the different peak shifts corresponding to the truly “grafted” polymer and that of the physically entrapped polymer. Additionally, 1H NMR can provide information on polymer yield (moles of polymer grafted per membrane mass) [25], presence of residual solvent, or degradation of the membrane.

content: provide information on polymer yield (moles of polymer grafted per membrane mass) [25], presence of residual solvent, or degradation of the membrane. Complementary techniques to NMR, like matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-­TOF-MS) can provide quantitative chemical information about macro­molecules and functional polymers. This technique has been used to quantify chemical modifications to end groups of polyethersulfones synthesized by chain growth [74,75]. Sample preparation for MALDI-TOF-MS involves dissolving the functional polymer in a solvent with ionizing agents whereas sample preparation for NMR involves dissolving the whole membrane in a deuterated solvent. In this contribution, we clearly demonstrate how current literatureaccepted experimental protocols and characterization methods can lead to ‘false positives’ of UV grafting from PES membranes. Methac­rylate monomers are commonly used by the membrane community to modify the surface of membranes [32], therefore, butyl methacrylate (BuMA) was selected as a model monomer. Polymerization reactions were conducted by direct photoinitiation of PES at 254 nm [30,32,50,51] using two different UV light intensities (7.97 ± 0.41 mW/cm2 and 21 ± 0.2 mW/cm2) and a range of monomer concentrations (0.001 wt%

content: - 24 wt%). Additionally, polymerization in the presence of AIBN at 365 nm was performed to be consistent with many literature reports [28,30,50,51,76]. The ability of NMR to show a distinct chemical signal for covalent PES attachment was demonstrated by using nucleophilic addition to covalently graft a polythiol from a PES membrane. The UV-initiated mechanism was further investigated in a solution-phase reaction in which poly(BuMA) was grafted from the monomeric unit of PES, 4,4′-sulfonylbis(phenoxybenzene). Thus, this work lays the foundation for adding NMR to the portfolio of membrane character­ization techniques, highlights the limitations of current literature-accepted characterization techniques, and identifies potential pitfalls in experimental design for UV-induced grafting. 2. Experimental section 2.1. Materials and reagents Deionized (DI) water was produced using a RiOS-DI3 Water Purifi­cation System (Millipore Sigma, CWRU) or a Synergy UV Water Purifi­cation System (Millipore Sigma, UA). Butyl methacrylate (BuMA) (99 %, stabilized with 4-methoxyphenol) and ethanol (99.5 %) were purchased from Acros Organics. 2,2′-Azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN, 98 %), aluminum oxide (activated, basic, Brockmann I), dimethyl sulfoxide-D6 (DMSO‑d6, MagniSolv deuteration degree min. 99.8 %) were pur­chased from Sigma Aldrich. The DMSO‑d6 (99.9 %) purchased from Cambridge Isotope laboratories was used for analysis. Denatured ethyl alcohol (90 %) was obtained from Fisher Scientific.

Millipore Express PLUS 0.45 μm polyethersulfone (PES) membranes (47 mm diameter) were used as received in all experiments. 2.2. UV light sources Two UV light sources were used to irradiate PES membranes. The first is a benchtop UV lamp (AnalytikJena UVP XX-15M) at Case Western Reserve University. Two bulb types were used in this study: a 254 nm mercury bulb (AnalytikJena part number 34-0008-01) and a 365 nm mercury 77bulb (AnalytikJena part number 34-0009-02). The mem­branes were positioned 12 cm below the UV lamp in all benchtop UV irradiations. The radiant flux (mW/cm2) of the 254 nm and 365 nm bulbs were measured with a UVC radiometer (Sper scientific, 220 nm–275 nm range) and a UVA/B radiometer (Sper Scientific, 290 nm–370 nm range), respectively. The average combined UVA and UVB radiant flux (for one bulb) was 12.36 mW/cm2 ± 0.68 mW/cm2. The average UVC-flux (for two bulbs) was 7.97 mW/cm2 ± 0.41 mW/cm2. Averages were calculated from 4 flux measurements made at different lateral positions underneath the UV lamp, shown in Fig. S1. The second UV light source is a UVACUBE 2000 (Honle UV America, Inc) at The University of Alabama. The useful working capacity of the chamber is 450 mm * 400 mm x 300 mm (height x width x depth), with the membrane samples sitting at the bottom of the chamber which is 450 mm from the light source.

The UVACUBE 2000 is equipped with a 150 mm arc length Hg lamp with a 133 W/cm arc power output. The system can operate at a lamp power of 2000 W or 1000 W. In this study, 1000 W was chosen because the UVC flux (20.8 mW/cm2 ± 0.6 mW/ cm2) matches closely with other reported studies [30,32,76,77]. The sampling locations for the UV flux measurements are shown in Fig. S2. 2.3. Membrane modification using a 254 UV light source Herein, we describe experimental protocols that mimic literature reports which intended to achieve true grafting from membranes, i.e., covalently attachment of polymer chains to the surface. Membranes did not contain pore filler and therefore were not rinsed before modification. 2.3.1. Monomer solution preparation Polymerization inhibitor (4-methoxyphenol) was removed from BuMA by passing the monomer through an activated alumina column. The alumina column was prepared by filling ~2 g of activated alumina (column height 7 cm) in a glass Pasteur pipet plugged with cotton or glass wool.

content: The alumina column was prepared by filling ~2 g of activated alumina (column height 7 cm) in a glass Pasteur pipet plugged with cotton or glass wool. Then, monomer solutions (0.001 wt% - 24 wt%) were pre­pared by dissolving the desired mass of uninhibited BuMA in reagent grade ethanol in a glass jar with a threaded cap. The capped jars con­taining BuMA solutions were placed on a magnetic stir plate (MS-M-S10, Scilogex, Connecticut (Case Western Reserve University) or (G-Bio­sciences magnetic stirrer BT1015, Fisher Scientific (The University of Alabama)) and wrapped in aluminum foil to prevent polymerization from being induced by ambient light. Solutions were stirred for at least 30 min prior to use in the UV polymerization reaction. To limit selfpolymerization of BuMA, the monomer solution was used within 3 h of removing inhibitors. 2.3.2. UV polymerization using the benchtop UV lamp and the immersion method Each PES membrane was placed dull side up in a glass Petri dish, then 3 mL of the monomer solution was pipetted onto the membrane. A plastic or rubber O-ring was placed atop the membrane, only touching the outer circumference of the membrane to prevent the membrane from floating or curling. The submerged membrane was soaked in the BuMA monomer solution for 15 min in a dark box. Then, the Petri dish con­taining the submerged membrane was irradiated using the 254 nm benchtop UV lamp containing two 254 nm bulbs for 3 h.

The total UV energy incident on the membrane during the reaction was 1153.42 J. Importantly, the membrane was not pressed between two petri dishes as borosilicate glass attenuates UV light below 250 nm [84]. During operation, reflective aluminum foil was draped over the UV lamp, and the experiment was enclosed by an opaque box to prevent human exposure to UV light. Membrane reaction conditions are summarized in Table 1 in the results and discussion. 2.3.3. UV polymerization using the UVACUBE 2000 reactor and the immersion method To investigate the effect of UV flux on the UV polymerization reac­tion, the UVACUBE 2000 was used for some membrane irradiations. As described in Section 2.2, the UVACUBE 2000 has a radiant flux of ~3 times that of the benchtop lamp. Importantly, the flux is the same as the Rayonet Reactor commonly used in the UV grafting literature [30,32,76,77]. Membranes were prepared according to the methods described in Section 2.3.1 then placed inside the UVACUBE 2000. The door to the UVACUBE 2000 remained closed during operation to prevent human exposure to UV light. Irradiation time was maintained at 10 min, which is consistent with other UV grafting procedures that use reactors with similar radiant fluxes (21 mW/cm2). The total UV energy incident on the membrane during the reaction was 168.84 J. 2.3.4.

Membrane washing Consistent with the literature, after UV irradiation, membranes were rinsed thoroughly using denatured ethanol to remove physisorbed polymer and the unreacted monomer. In this work, after rinsing with denatured ethanol, each functionalized membrane was submerged in 30 mL denatured ethanol in a 50 mL centrifuge tube. Centrifuge tubes were capped and placed on a shaker table (Lab-Line Environ-Shaker, Orbit) to shake overnight at 120 RPM - 150 RPM and room temperature. After shaking, membranes were dried 16 h in a vacuum oven (Fisher-brand Isotemp) at 95 ◦C and under vacuum (P = 0.03 bar) before character­ization. Membranes were not dried before water permeance measurements.

content: 2.4. Membrane modification using a 365 nm UV light source Some studies include an initiator, like AIBN, to increase the degree of grafting. While typically not used as a photo-initiator on its own, AIBN absorbs UV light at 347 nm [56,78]; therefore, membranes prepared using the AIBN initiator were irradiated with the benchtop UV lamp containing one bulb at 365 nm. 2.4.1. Monomer and initiator solution preparation In addition to a BuMA monomer solution prepared according to Section 2.3.1, an initiator solution was prepared by dissolving AIBN in ethanol. The mass of AIBN was 6 wt% of the BuMA monomer mass in the monomer solution. Membranes were placed dull side up in Fisherbrand glass jars with threaded caps then 3 mL of the initiator solution was pipetted onto each membrane. The jars were capped, and membranes were soaked in the initiator solution for 1 h. After soaking, the mem­branes were removed from the jars and dried in the air for 1 h. 2.4.2. UV polymerization using the benchtop UV lamp and the immersion method The air-dried, initiator-loaded membranes were transferred to glass Petri dishes with the dull side facing up. Then, 3 mL of monomer solu­tion were pipetted onto the membrane. To prevent the membrane from floating or curling in the monomer solution, the membrane was sand­wiched underneath a smaller glass Petri dish. The glass Petri dish does not interfere with the radiant flux at 365 nm because borosilicate glass does not attenuate UV light above 250 nm.

content: 2.6.4. Degree of functionalization The degree of functionalization is a relative measure of accumulated polymer on the membrane. It can be measured gravimetrically, using peak ratios from the ATR-FTIR spectra, or using peak ratios from 1H NMR. This measure is commonly termed as ‘degree of grafting’; how­ever, considering the nature of this work, the term ‘degree of function­alization’ is used to avoid ambiguity. The gravimetric degree of functionalization (DFg) is estimated by weighing the membrane after functionalization, shown in Equation (1), where m1 and m2 are the masses of the membrane before and after the polymerization reaction, respectively. Functionalized membranes were weighed immediately after vacuum drying to obtain m2. DFg (%) = m2 -m1 m1 x 100Equation 1 Additionally, the degree of functionalization was estimated by calculating the ratio of two characteristic peaks in the ATR-FTIR spec­trum (DFATR), shown in Equation (2). IR peak ratios have been used in the literature to estimate the degree of grafting [59,76]. All membranes irradiated with 254 nm UV light exhibit some degradation as evidenced by pore collapse. The most pronounced morphological changes were observed for the membrane irradiated in the UVACUBE (70 min, λ = 254 nm) without monomer present (0wt-254nm-cube-70min). Membranes irra­diated with the UVACUBE were exposed to ~3 times higher UV flux than the benchtop lamp. ATR-FTIR degree of functionalization (DFATR) ATR-FTIR is commonly used to support claims of grafting from a membrane surface. An illustrative example is shown in Fig. 1 for membranes irradiated in the UVACUBE (λ = 254 nm, 10 min) with varying monomer concentration (0.01 wt% – 24 wt%). Here, the char­acteristic absorbance of C––O moiety of the methacrylate-based mono­mer appears at 1730 cm-1. A plausible scenario could involve the polymerization of poly(BuMA) in solution and subsequent surface adsorption. In that case, no covalent bonds are formed between the PES membrane and poly(BuMA). The direct evidence for grafting in ATR-FTIR would be a new C–C peak which would be indistinguishable from others in the fingerprint region or a new SO2–C peak which would be indistinguishable from the initial PES SO2–C peak. 3.1.4.

Summary of potential pitfalls in membrane characterization As the premise of this paper is to identify potential pitfalls in UVmodified membrane characterization, we will summarize in­consistencies in the data using the metrics described above. First, analyzing the degree of functionalization gravimetrically (DFg) suggests that 6 out of 9 membranes modified in this study lost mass. Several phenomena may be occurring simultaneously during the membrane functionalization reaction including PES chain scission by the UV light, membrane degradation (as seen in Fig. S3), dissolution of oligomers, and polymer grafting. Thus, the DFg measurement is convoluted by these contributions—rendering it an unclear metric to support functionalization. Second, analyzing the DFATR (as measured by ATR-FTIR) suggests that the degree of functionalization generally increases with increasing concentration for the 254 nm-cube-10min series, Fig. 1. Within that series, comparing the 1 wt% membrane to the 24 wt% membrane, it would appear that increasing the concentration increases the amount of polymer on the surface. As mentioned previously, DFATR is an indirect measurement of DF, and it cannot differentiate between physisorbed and grafted polymer. On the other hand, the degree of functionalization calculated by NMR (DFNMR) is a direct measure of the molar composition of the membrane.

It also highlights in­consistencies in the DFATR data. For example, the DFATR of the 24wt254nm-cube-10min membrane was 19.21 % and the DFNMR was 0.87 %. The overestimation of DF by the ATR-FTIR could be due to the physics of the ATR-FTIR instrument in which the evanescent wave reflected in the membrane has a low depth of penetration. For the single bounce diamond crystal used in this work, that depth of penetration is approximately 2 μm. Since UV-initiated free radical polymerization is a surface modification technique, as more functional polymer is added to the surface, the characteristic peak in­tensity will increase.

content: 3.2.2. Methacrylate monomers absorb UV light and may shield PES from UV activation Pieracciet al. [59] grafted N-vinyl pyrrolidone (NVP) (1 wt% - 5 wt %) from PES ultrafiltration membranes using both dip and immersion modification techniques. They concluded that the immersion modifi­cation technique resulted in lower degrees of grafting (DG) than the dip technique and supported that conclusion using ATR-FTIR. NVP has a high UV absorbance at 254 nm and may shield the membrane from the UV light, ultimately limiting the activation of PES. The authors suggest that the grafting reaction could compete with the homopolymerization of NVP, leading to a lower DG. Thus, to enable the UV-grafting mech­anism, procedural elements such as the UV absorbance of monomers and solvent, monomer concentration, and intensity of the UV light must be considered. To explore the consequences of UV shielding effects, a range of monomer concentrations used in this study (as low as 0.001 wt%) were analyzed using UV-Visible Spectroscopy. The BuMA monomer was dis­solved in reagent-grade ethanol at varying concentrations to mimic experimental conditions. PES does not dissolve in ethanol; therefore, it was dissolved in DMSO to measure the UV absorbance. The UV absor­bances of BuMA and PES are shown in Fig. 4. Exploring the use of NMR to collect direct evidence of covalent ‘grafting from’ PES membranes Until now, we have identified several characterization and proce­dural pitfalls that should be avoided when functionalizing membranes using UV initiation. Now, we aim to prove the reaction mechanism where PES is activated by UV light and initiates polymerization. To probe the covalent attachment of BuMA to the PES membrane, the modified membranes were characterized by 1H and HMBC NMR. To have direct evidence of covalent attachment the following evidence are necessary: 1) scission of PES chains, and 2) presence of poly(BuMA) with covalent coupling as determined by HMBC. content: 3.3.3. 2D NMR (HMBC) can provide evidence of covalent attachment of BuMA to PES In this part of the work, we designed an experiment to provide direct evidence that BuMA can form a covalent bond with PES. To simplify interpretation and purification, 4,4′-sulfonylbis(phenoxybenzene) was used as a representative PES monomer and the reaction was performed in solution. The 4,4′-sulfonylbis(phenoxybenzene) was dissolved in DMSO‑d6 containing BuMA (1:2 PES:BuMA mol ratio). To put this in perspective, a typical 24 wt% reaction performed on the membranes had ~1:20 PES:BuMA mole ratio. The reaction mixture was irradiated under the 254 nm lamp at ambient conditions for 16 h. The reaction scheme is given in Fig. 8. Then, the reaction mixture was concentrated by blowing air and evaporating excess solvent, and the reaction products were separated via column chromatography. One of the fractions, when analyzed using 1H NMR, showed evidence of both PES and BuMA moieties. PES peaks occur only within the aromatic region (6.5 ppm–8 ppm) and BuMA peaks occur only within the aliphatic region (4.5 ppm–0 ppm) of a 1H NMR spectrum. The fraction containing peaks in both regions was further analyzed by 2D NMR to investigate whether a covalent bond was formed between the BuMA and representative PES monomer. The peaks of the 1H NMR spectrum arising from the BuMA moiety can be unambiguously assigned as in Fig. 2. Protons Ha1 and Ha2 are diastereotopic, each giving rise to a doublet (2.90 ppm and 2.83 ppm).

content: BuMA moiety can be unambiguously assigned as in Fig. 2. Protons Ha1 and Ha2 are diastereotopic, each giving rise to a doublet (2.90 ppm and 2.83 ppm). They are chemically unique due to their positions relative to the chiral center, Cb. Unambiguous assignments of the carbon peaks are enabled using Heteronuclear Multiple-Quantum Correlation spectroscopy (HMQC) (Fig. S9). Each peak in the HMQC spectrum arises from the transfer of magnetization of the excited 1H nucleus to the 13C nucleus through the one-bond attachment. The HMQC spectrum shows a direct attachment of Ha1 and Ha2 to the carbon peak at 45.2 ppm, allowing its assignment as Ca. The remaining carbons can be assigned similarly, except for quaternary Cb and carbonyl Cd, which have no one-bond C–H couplings. The HMBC spectrum allows for assignments based on further connectivities, two-to three-bond couplings (Fig. 9). Protons Ha1 and Ha2 show four peaks in the HMBC spectrum, representing couplings to four different carbon nuclei. The first coupling is to the carbon at 25.8 ppm, which is Cc. Protons Ha1 and Ha2 have couplings to the carbons at 74.9 ppm and 175.7 ppm which Hc also shares; these are quaternary Cb and carbonyl Cd, respectively. Most interesting are the coupling of Ha1 and Ha2 with the carbon at 132.2 ppm. Coupling of a BuMA proton with a carbon in the aromatic region for 13C spectra indicates that the BuMA molecule has covalently bonded with a benzene ring from the PES moiety.

The HMQC shows that this carbon has an attached proton, the doublet at 7.19 ppm. These are assigned C2 and H2, respectively. Proton H2 shows the reciprocal coupling to Ca at 45.2 ppm. Proton H3 at 6.89 ppm shows HMBC coupling to C2, allowing its assignment and, from its HMQC coupling to the carbon at 118.5 ppm, C3. Proton H2 shows a strong coupling to the carbon at 155.5 ppm, to which H3 shows a weak coupling; this is assigned to C4. Surprisingly, no peaks in the HMBC spectrum are indicative of C1, despite expected couplings from Ha1, Ha2, H2, and H3. However, only BuMA which has covalently bonded to a PES section could show such aliphatic to aromatic couplings, as are present in the HMBC spectrum showing Ha1 and Ha2 coupling to C2 and H2 coupling to Ca. Importantly, these two couplings show the covalent attachment of BuMA to 4,4′-sulfonylbis(phenoxybenzene) and provide the first direct evidence of the proposed reaction mechanism for UVgrafting [68]. No such direct evidence has been collected for the het­erogeneous reaction (PES and monomer). Though, without rigorous purification of the functionalized PES polymer, the concentration of the surface-localized products may be below the detection limits of the NMR. 4.

Conclusions In summary, PES membranes were functionalized with butyl meth­acrylate (BuMA) using UV-initiated free radical polymerization at varying concentrations and UV fluxes. This work demonstrates that traditional characterization techniques can cause misinterpretations of successfully ‘grafting from’ PES membranes, a.k.a. false positives. Several procedural pitfalls were identified. First, using gravimetric methods and ATR-FTIR peak ratios for measuring the degree of “graft­ing” are not accurate, since ATR-FTIR cannot differentiate between unreacted monomer, covalently grafted polymer, and physisorbed polymer. Second, membrane properties such as water contact angle and water sorption can be altered simply by contact with ethanol, which may be misinterpreted as an effect of a grafted polymer. Thus, solvent treated controls are necessary for all contact angle and water uptake measure­ments. Third, thorough washing of membranes using sonication is an efficient method for removing physisorbed polymer. Fourth, methacry­late monomers absorb UV light at 254 nm and absorption increases with concentration—potentially shielding the PES membranes from the UV. This trend also was supported by SEM in which membrane degradation was inversely proportional to the concentration of the monomer solu­tion.

When designing a UV-grafting reaction, the absorbance of mono­mer and solvent must be considered. Finally, the UV flux (mW/cm2) is more important for PES activation than the accumulated UV energy (J) incident on the membrane.

content: Here, 1H NMR and 2D NMR were introduced to quantify poly (BUMA) on the membrane and pursue direct evidence of covalent attachment between PES and BuMA. 1H NMR can be used to quantify the molar ratios of polymer, monomer, and PES membrane. Further, 1H NMR provides key evidence of grafting-from the membrane: aromatic peak broadening due to chain fragmentation and chemical shifts due to the presence of new functional groups. So far, covalent attachment of poly(BuMA) onto a 4,4′-sulfonylbis(phenoxybenzene) (PES monomer) has only been achieved and detected in the solution phase. The 1H NMR and 2D NMR of this reaction were presented. HMBC shows evidence for direct coupling of 4,4′-sulfonylbis(phenoxybenzene) to BuMA providing the first direct evidence of this reaction mechanism. The difference between covalently bonded polymer and physi­osorbed polymer may not be significant for all applications. However, for applications that depend on polymer or brush structure and mobility for separation, or in applications that require repeated cycling or cleaning protocols, this difference may be crucial, as physiosorbed polymers could be released from the membrane surface during these processes. Adding NMR to the membrane characterization portfolio would provide quantitative information for degrees of functionalizing and unambiguous evidence of ‘grafting from’.
